questions.stem: You are reviewing a teammate's code and find a method named handleStuff(). Which naming rule do you cite to ask for a rename?
questions.explanation: 'handleStuff' tells the reader nothing about why it exists or what it does, so it fails the intention-revealing-names rule (CleanCode p.11). It is not a type encoding, a synonym clash, or a context issue. (CleanCode p.11)

questions.stem: You are about to delete a block of code but worry you might need it later, so you comment it out. What does Clean Code advise?

options.text: Wrap it in a TODO comment
Keep it commented with your initials
Delete it — version control remembers it
Move it to a journal comment at the top

questions.explanation: Commented-out code is a smell; delete it because version control preserves history (CleanCode p.47). Attributions, journal comments, and TODOs are themselves discouraged or unrelated. (CleanCode p.47)

questions.stem: Which is NOT a property in the F.I.R.S.T. acronym for clean tests?
questions.explanation: F.I.R.S.T. = Fast, Independent, Repeatable, Self-validating, Timely (CleanCode p.77). 'Formal' is not part of it. (CleanCode p.77)

questions.stem: Which of these is NOT a class-name style the deck recommends?

options.text: A concrete domain noun like WikiPage
A verb phrase like Manage or Process

questions.explanation: Class names should be nouns/noun phrases and 'should not be a verb'; vague verbs like Manage/Process are discouraged (CleanCode p.18).

questions.stem: Which is NOT one of the Three Laws of TDD?

options.text: Write no more production code than passes the current test
You may delete tests once the code compiles
Write no production code until a failing test exists
Write no more test than is sufficient to fail

questions.explanation: The Three Laws are about test-first, minimal failing test, minimal passing code (CleanCode p.74). Deleting tests after compiling is not a law. (CleanCode p.74)

questions.stem: Order the monadic forms the deck lists for a single-argument function.

options.text: Return an error code; mutate a global; print a log
Ask a question; transform and return; raise an event

questions.explanation: The three monadic forms are ask-a-question, transform-and-return, and event (CleanCode p.28).

questions.stem: You are naming a method that posts a payment. Per the deck, method names should be:
questions.explanation: Method names should be verbs or verb phrases like postPayment (CleanCode p.19).

questions.stem: You are naming a class that represents a customer's address. Per the deck, class names should be:
questions.explanation: Class names should be nouns/noun phrases like Address, not verbs (CleanCode p.18).

questions.stem: The deck says code formatting exists chiefly to serve which goal?
questions.explanation: Formatting is communication, a professional courtesy to readers (CleanCode p.53).

questions.stem: TDD's First Law bars one specific action until something else exists. Which rule does it impose?

options.text: Write tests only for public methods
Write all tests after the code is complete
Don't write production code until a failing unit test exists

questions.explanation: First Law: no production code until a failing test exists (CleanCode p.74).

questions.stem: If you had to name the single most important quality of a clean test per the deck, which would it be?
questions.explanation: Clean tests are about 'readability, readability, and readability' (CleanCode p.75).

questions.stem: An intention-revealing name should answer which three questions?

options.text: Why it exists, what it does, and how it is used
Who wrote it, when, and why

questions.explanation: An intention-revealing name answers why it exists, what it does, and how it's used (CleanCode p.11).

questions.stem: The Boy Scout Rule is the personal-habit form of which broader practice?

options.text: Incremental code improvement (refactoring as you pass through)

questions.explanation: The Boy Scout Rule expresses incremental improvement — leave code cleaner than you found it (CleanCode p.3).

questions.stem: What does 'searchable names' mean in practice?

options.text: Names stored in a search index
Names and constants that can be found with a text search, unlike single letters or magic numbers

questions.explanation: Searchable names replace ungreppable single letters and magic numbers (CleanCode p.14).

questions.stem: Asked to capture the Law of Demeter in a single memorable line, which of these is the deck's chosen summary?

options.text: Talk only to your immediate friends; don't talk to strangers

questions.explanation: Demeter: talk only to immediate friends, avoiding train wrecks (CleanCode p.64).

questions.stem: In error handling, what role does the Special Case pattern play?

options.text: Returns null for the special case
Returns an object encoding the special case so the normal flow stays clean
Logs the special case and continues

questions.explanation: The Special Case pattern returns an object that handles the case so callers have no special branch (CleanCode p.69-70). (CleanCode p.69)

questions.stem: Among the package coupling principles, the abbreviation SDP unpacks to which name?
questions.explanation: SDP = Stable Dependencies Principle (DesignPrinciplesAndPatterns p.22).

questions.stem: Among the package coupling principles, the abbreviation SAP unpacks to which name?
questions.explanation: SAP = Stable Abstractions Principle (DesignPrinciplesAndPatterns p.24).

questions.stem: Among the package coupling principles, the abbreviation ADP unpacks to which name?
questions.explanation: ADP = Acyclic Dependencies Principle (DesignPrinciplesAndPatterns p.18).

questions.stem: What does REP stand for among the cohesion principles?
questions.explanation: REP = Release Reuse Equivalency Principle (DesignPrinciplesAndPatterns p.17).

questions.stem: What does CCP stand for among the cohesion principles?
questions.explanation: CCP = Common Closure Principle (DesignPrinciplesAndPatterns p.17).

questions.stem: What does CRP stand for among the cohesion principles?
questions.explanation: CRP = Common Reuse Principle (DesignPrinciplesAndPatterns p.17).

questions.stem: Finish the deck's three-part slogan: functions should do one thing, do it well, and do it ___. Which word fills the blank?
questions.explanation: '...do it only' (CleanCode p.23).

questions.stem: Replacing the magic numbers in for (int j=0; j<34; ...) s += (t[j]*4)/5; with named constants chiefly honours which naming rule?
questions.explanation: Named constants make magic numbers searchable (CleanCode p.14).

questions.stem: What does the Second Law of TDD add to 'write no more test than is sufficient to fail'?

options.text: Tests must be deleted after passing

questions.explanation: The Second Law clarifies that not compiling is failing (CleanCode p.74).

questions.stem: What does the Third Law of TDD limit?

options.text: Production code to no more than passes the current failing test
The number of tests per class

questions.explanation: Third Law: write only enough production code to pass the current test (CleanCode p.74).

questions.stem: Which word best completes the deck's ideal: a function should do one thing, do it well, and do it ____?
questions.explanation: The slogan ends '...do it only' (CleanCode p.23).

questions.stem: A name you cannot say out loud in a code review fails which Meaningful-Names rule?
questions.explanation: Names you can't pronounce can't be discussed — use pronounceable names (CleanCode p.13).

questions.stem: A boolean parameter passed into a function is commonly called a what?
questions.explanation: A boolean parameter is a flag argument, a code smell (CleanCode p.29).

questions.stem: Which describes the deck's preferred replacement for returning null from a collection method?
questions.explanation: Return an empty collection instead of null (CleanCode p.71-72). (CleanCode p.71)

questions.stem: Per the deck, accessor, mutator and predicate methods follow which prefixes?
questions.explanation: Accessors/mutators/predicates use get/set/is (CleanCode p.19).

questions.stem: Which of these is the deck's term for a name that misleads, such as l versus 1?
questions.explanation: A misleading name is disinformation (CleanCode p.12).

questions.stem: A function with exactly one argument is called what?
questions.explanation: One argument is monadic (CleanCode p.27).

questions.stem: A function with exactly two arguments is called what?
questions.explanation: Two arguments is dyadic (CleanCode p.30).

questions.stem: Which rule says a function should EITHER do something OR answer something, not both?
questions.explanation: Command/Query Separation: do or answer, not both (CleanCode p.32).

questions.stem: Per the deck, what should you do with bad code instead of explaining it in a comment?
questions.explanation: 'Don't comment bad code — rewrite it' (CleanCode p.34).

questions.stem: A copyright or license header is which kind of comment?
questions.explanation: Copyright/license headers are legal comments, a good category (CleanCode p.35).

questions.stem: Dead code left inside a comment block is which smell?
questions.explanation: Commented-out code is a smell; delete it (CleanCode p.47).

questions.stem: The standard class organization places which group at the very top?
questions.explanation: Public static constants come first (CleanCode p.78).

questions.stem: Which principle states 'a class should have one, and only one, reason to change'?
questions.explanation: One reason to change is SRP (CleanCode p.79).

questions.stem: Which symptom names software that is hard to change because edits cascade?
questions.explanation: Hard-to-change-with-cascades is rigidity (DesignPrinciplesAndPatterns p.2).

questions.stem: Which symptom names software that breaks in many, often unrelated, places when changed?
questions.explanation: Unexpected breakage is fragility (DesignPrinciplesAndPatterns p.2).

questions.stem: Which formula gives a package's instability I?
questions.explanation: Instability I = Ce/(Ca+Ce) (DesignPrinciplesAndPatterns p.23).

questions.stem: The Observer pattern's two core roles are:
questions.explanation: Observer's roles are Subject and Observer (DesignPrinciplesAndPatterns p.30).

questions.stem: Which pattern's whole job is to translate-and-delegate to a server you cannot modify?
questions.explanation: Adapter translates and delegates to an unmodifiable server (DesignPrinciplesAndPatterns p.29).

questions.stem: Which pattern is creational in the GoF taxonomy?
questions.explanation: Abstract Factory is creational (DesignPrinciplesAndPatterns p.32).

questions.stem: What is the governing ethic the deck opens with about leaving code?
questions.explanation: The Boy Scout Rule — leave code cleaner than you found it (CleanCode p.3).

questions.stem: A test that gives a clear pass/fail with no manual interpretation has which FIRST property?
questions.explanation: A boolean pass/fail is the Self-validating property (CleanCode p.77).

questions.stem: Per the deck, what is the ideal upper bound on a function's length?
questions.explanation: Functions should be small, under about 20 lines (CleanCode p.23).

questions.stem: A blank line between two blocks of code is, per the deck, a visual cue for what?
questions.explanation: Each blank line signals a new, separate concept (vertical openness) (CleanCode p.53).

questions.stem: You are cleaning a 60-line function that loads a config, parses it, and renders HTML. Which Clean Code rule most directly tells you to break it up?

options.text: Functions should be small and do one thing

questions.explanation: A function doing three jobs across 60 lines violates 'small / do one thing' (CleanCode p.23). The other rules target errors, names and null, not size/responsibility. (CleanCode p.23)

questions.stem: While refactoring, you find render(boolean isSuite). You want to remove the boolean. What does Clean Code tell you to do?

options.text: Add a Javadoc explaining the two modes
Split it into renderForSuite() and renderForSingleTest()
Wrap the boolean in an argument object
Rename the parameter to a more descriptive name

questions.explanation: A flag argument signals the function does more than one thing; the fix is to split it into two intention-revealing functions (CleanCode p.29). Renaming, wrapping, or documenting the flag leaves the two-jobs smell intact. (CleanCode p.29)

questions.stem: You are tidying a function and find a magic literal 86400 used three times. Which Meaningful-Names rule applies?
questions.explanation: Raw numeric literals can't be grep'd and carry no meaning; replacing 86400 with a named constant satisfies 'use searchable names' (CleanCode p.14). The other rules address translation, speech, and over-qualification. (CleanCode p.14)

questions.stem: You are mid-refactor and notice the same validation block copy-pasted into four methods. Which single rule most directly condemns this?
questions.explanation: Copy-pasted logic is duplication, which DRY calls 'the root of all evil in software' (CleanCode p.33). CQS, the Stepdown Rule, and flag arguments address other smells. (CleanCode p.33)

questions.stem: You are cleaning a class and find getX(), getY() exposing raw fields plus a saveToDatabase() method. Which clean-design idea warns against this mix?

options.text: Data/object anti-symmetry — don't make a thing both a data structure and an object

questions.explanation: Exposing data like a structure while also carrying behaviour like an object blurs the data/object anti-symmetry (CleanCode p.63). Demeter, CQS, and Stepdown address chains, dual-purpose calls, and ordering. (CleanCode p.63)

questions.stem: You are renaming variables in a loop that uses a and b as counters. Clean Code says i and j would be better here. Why?

options.text: a/b are reserved words in Java
i/j are an established convention, so they avoid mental mapping
i/j are more searchable than a/b

questions.explanation: i/j are a recognised loop-counter convention, so they avoid forcing the reader to translate; a/b create mental mapping (CleanCode p.17). They are not reserved, single letters aren't banned outright, and neither is searchable. (CleanCode p.17)

questions.stem: You are cleaning code that calls ctxt.getOptions().getScratchDir().getAbsolutePath(). A reviewer calls it a 'train wreck.' Which rule are they invoking?
questions.explanation: A long navigation chain through object graphs is the classic Law-of-Demeter train wreck (CleanCode p.64). CQS, SRP, and DRY target dual-purpose calls, responsibilities, and duplication. (CleanCode p.64)

questions.stem: You find a method set(attr, value) that returns a boolean, used as if (set("user","bob")). What clean-code fix removes the ambiguity?

options.text: Add a flag argument to choose set vs check
Separate the query from the command (e.g. attributeExists then setAttribute)
Wrap the arguments in an object

questions.explanation: The method both acts and answers, violating Command/Query Separation; split it into a query and a command (CleanCode p.32). A flag, a rename, or an argument object does not resolve the do-or-answer ambiguity. (CleanCode p.32)

questions.stem: You are cleaning a function that mixes a high-level getHtml() call, an intermediate parse call, and a low-level .append("\n"). Which rule do you apply?

options.text: Keep one level of abstraction per function
Use exceptions instead of error codes

questions.explanation: Blending high, intermediate, and low abstraction in one function violates 'one level of abstraction per function' (CleanCode p.24). Flags, error handling, and naming context are different concerns. (CleanCode p.24)

questions.stem: You are cleaning a constructor-heavy class with overloaded constructors. The deck suggests a more readable creation idiom. Which?

options.text: A builder that returns null on failure
Public fields set directly by callers
Static factory methods with descriptive names (Complex.fromRealNumber)
A single constructor taking a flag argument

questions.explanation: For overloaded constructors, named static factory methods like Complex.fromRealNumber(23.0) are 'generally better than' new Complex(23.0) (CleanCode p.19). Flags, null returns, and public fields are anti-patterns. (CleanCode p.19)

questions.stem: You are cleaning a function with the comment '// Check to see if the employee is eligible for full benefits' above a flag-bit test. The deck's preferred fix is to:

options.text: Expand the comment into full Javadoc
Keep the comment as an amplification
Replace it with employee.isEligibleForFullBenefits() and delete the comment
Move the comment below the code

questions.explanation: 'Explain yourself in code': extract a self-naming method like isEligibleForFullBenefits() so the comment becomes unnecessary (CleanCode p.34). Expanding, moving, or relabelling the comment misses the point. (CleanCode p.34)

questions.stem: Which of the following is NOT one of the four Rules of Simple Design (Emergence)?
questions.explanation: The four rules are: runs all tests, no duplication, expressive, minimal classes/methods (CleanCode p.80). A 90% coverage target is not one of them. (CleanCode p.80)

questions.stem: Which of these is NOT a good-comment category in the Clean Code deck?
questions.explanation: Legal, intent, and warning comments are good; journal/change-log comments are a bad category that belongs in version control (CleanCode p.42).

questions.stem: Which of the following is NOT one of the deck's three legitimate uses of a single (monadic) argument?

options.text: Operating on it and returning a transformed result
Returning an error code about the argument
Asking a question about it (boolean fileExists)

questions.explanation: The monadic forms are: ask a question, transform-and-return, and event (CleanCode p.28). Returning an error code is not among them; the deck prefers exceptions to error codes. (CleanCode p.28)

questions.stem: Which of the following is NOT one of Martin's four symptoms of rotting design?
questions.explanation: The four symptoms are rigidity, fragility, immobility, and viscosity (DesignPrinciplesAndPatterns p.2-3). Redundancy is not on that list. (DesignPrinciplesAndPatterns p.2)

questions.stem: Which is NOT advice the deck gives about function arguments?

options.text: Three arguments are ideal because they cover most cases
Wrap many arguments in an object

questions.explanation: The ideal argument count is zero, with triads to be avoided (CleanCode p.27). Claiming three is ideal inverts the rule. (CleanCode p.27)

questions.stem: Which is NOT one of the three package COHESION principles?
questions.explanation: The cohesion principles are REP, CCP, CRP; ADP is a coupling principle (DesignPrinciplesAndPatterns p.16-18). (DesignPrinciplesAndPatterns p.17)

questions.stem: Which is NOT one of the three package COUPLING principles?
questions.explanation: The coupling principles are ADP, SDP, SAP; CCP is a cohesion principle (DesignPrinciplesAndPatterns p.18-25). (DesignPrinciplesAndPatterns p.18)

questions.stem: Which is NOT one of the five patterns named in the DesignPrinciplesAndPatterns deck?
questions.explanation: The deck names Abstract Server, Adapter, Observer, Bridge, and Abstract Factory; Singleton is not among them (DesignPrinciplesAndPatterns p.28-32). (DesignPrinciplesAndPatterns p.28)

questions.stem: Which statement is NOT true of the Open/Closed Principle?

options.text: You should modify existing source code to add each new behaviour

questions.explanation: OCP's whole point is to add behaviour without modifying working source; you extend via abstraction (DesignPrinciplesAndPatterns p.4). Modifying source for each new case is the violation it prevents. (DesignPrinciplesAndPatterns p.4)

questions.stem: Which is NOT part of the deck's standard class organization order?

options.text: Each private utility right after the public function that uses it
Private instance variables placed after all public functions

questions.explanation: The order is public static constants -> private static variables -> private instance variables -> public functions, with helpers after their callers (CleanCode p.78). Instance variables come before, not after, the public functions. (CleanCode p.78)

questions.stem: Which of the following does the deck NOT list among good comments?
questions.explanation: TODO, clarification, and public-API Javadoc are good; position-marker banners are a bad comment (CleanCode p.46).

questions.stem: Which is NOT a formatting idea the deck endorses?

options.text: Aligning columns of variable declarations for neatness

questions.explanation: Martin actually discourages aligning declaration columns; ordinary indentation suffices (CleanCode p.57-58). Vertical openness, the newspaper metaphor, and team rules are endorsed. (CleanCode p.57)

questions.stem: OCP and DIP are closely linked. Which statement captures their relationship per the deck?

options.text: OCP is a restatement of LSP
DIP states the goal; OCP states the metric
OCP states the goal of OO architecture; DIP states the primary mechanism

questions.explanation: 'If the OCP states the goal of OO architecture, the DIP states the primary mechanism' (DesignPrinciplesAndPatterns p.12).

questions.stem: 'Don't return null' and 'don't pass null' are presented as two separate rules. What is the key difference?

options.text: One is about tests, the other about formatting
They are the same rule stated twice
One forbids null as a return value; the other forbids null as an argument
One applies to collections, the other to exceptions

questions.explanation: Returning null burdens callers with checks; passing null burdens the callee with defenses — distinct rules (CleanCode p.71-73). (CleanCode p.71)

questions.stem: Compare objects and data structures per data/object anti-symmetry. Which kind of change does each make easy?

options.text: Objects make adding new types easy; data structures make adding new functions easy
Both make adding new functions easy
Both make adding new types easy
Objects make adding new functions easy; data structures make adding new types easy

questions.explanation: OO/object code eases adding new types; procedural/data-structure code eases adding new functions — they are opposites (CleanCode p.63).

questions.stem: Adapter and Bridge are both GoF structural patterns. What separates their typical timing of use?

options.text: Adapter notifies observers; Bridge registers them
Adapter retrofits an interface onto an existing/unchangeable type; Bridge is designed in up front to let abstraction and implementation vary
Adapter is creational; Bridge is behavioral
Adapter quarantines object creation; Bridge wraps a third party

questions.explanation: Adapter is a retrofit for an existing server you can't change; Bridge is a deliberate up-front separation of abstraction and implementation (DesignPrinciplesAndPatterns p.29-31). (DesignPrinciplesAndPatterns p.30)

questions.stem: A pattern inserts an abstract interface between a Consumer and a concrete ResourceManager so different server implementations can be bound to an unsuspecting client. Name it.
questions.explanation: Inserting an interface as a 'hinge point' between client and a modifiable server is Abstract Server (DesignPrinciplesAndPatterns p.28).

questions.stem: A Sensor derives from a Subject that holds a list populated by Register(); a Meter implements Update(); on a reading change the Subject cycles observers calling Update. Name the pattern.
questions.explanation: Subject/Observer with Register and Update notification is the Observer pattern (DesignPrinciplesAndPatterns p.29-30). (DesignPrinciplesAndPatterns p.30)

questions.stem: An object implements an abstract interface and every one of its methods 'translates and then delegates' to an existing concrete server you may not modify. Name the pattern.
questions.explanation: Implementing the interface and delegating to an unmodifiable server is Adapter (DesignPrinciplesAndPatterns p.29).

questions.stem: Users call Make(string) on a global interface that returns a Modem*; only one class knows the concrete Hayes/Courrier/Ernie types, and only main knows that class. Name the pattern.
questions.explanation: Quarantining concrete-class creation behind a factory interface reached via a global is Abstract Factory (DesignPrinciplesAndPatterns p.32).

questions.stem: A MusicSynthesizer's PlayMidi is decoupled from EmitVoice by routing through a VoiceEmitter interface, so each can be implemented independently. Name the pattern.
questions.explanation: Separating an abstraction (PlayMidi) from its implementation (EmitVoice) via an interface so both vary independently is Bridge (DesignPrinciplesAndPatterns p.30-31). (DesignPrinciplesAndPatterns p.31)

questions.stem: A developer adds if (typeid(e) == typeid(Ellipse)) to a client to defend against a misbehaving Circle subtype. Name the principle this defensive check itself violates.

options.text: OCP (the fix is a latent OCP violation)

questions.explanation: Such type-checking defenses are latent OCP violations — they must be revisited for every new subtype (DesignPrinciplesAndPatterns p.11-12). (DesignPrinciplesAndPatterns p.12)

questions.stem: Given the violation: every change to a module triggers a cascade of edits in dependent modules, turning a two-day job into weeks. Name the symptom.
questions.explanation: Cascading edits that make even simple changes expensive is rigidity (DesignPrinciplesAndPatterns p.2).

questions.stem: Given the violation: a single change makes the system break in many places, often unrelated to the change. Name the symptom.
questions.explanation: Unexpected breakage in many, often unrelated places is fragility (DesignPrinciplesAndPatterns p.2).

questions.stem: Given the violation: a useful module can't be lifted into another project because it drags too many dependencies with it. Name the symptom.
questions.explanation: Inability to reuse because of dependency baggage is immobility (DesignPrinciplesAndPatterns p.3).

questions.stem: Given the violation: slow builds and slow source control tempt developers into design-degrading shortcuts. Name the (form of the) symptom.
questions.explanation: Slow tooling that encourages shortcuts is viscosity of environment (DesignPrinciplesAndPatterns p.3).

questions.stem: Given the rule: 'Many client-specific interfaces are better than one general-purpose interface.' Name the principle.
questions.explanation: Splitting a fat interface into client-specific ones is ISP (DesignPrinciplesAndPatterns p.14).

questions.stem: Given the rule: 'Depend upon abstractions; do not depend upon concretions.' Name the principle.
questions.explanation: Depending on abstractions rather than concretions is DIP (DesignPrinciplesAndPatterns p.12).

questions.stem: Given the rule: 'The granule of reuse is the granule of release.' Name the principle.
questions.explanation: Equating reuse units with release units is REP (DesignPrinciplesAndPatterns p.17).

questions.stem: Given the rule: 'Depend in the direction of stability.' Name the principle and its metric.

options.text: ADP — using the distance metric D'
SDP — using the instability metric I = Ce/(Ca+Ce)
SAP — using the abstractness metric A = Na/Nc

questions.explanation: Depending toward stability is SDP, governed by I = Ce/(Ca+Ce) (DesignPrinciplesAndPatterns p.22-24). (DesignPrinciplesAndPatterns p.23)

questions.stem: Given the rule: 'Stable packages should be abstract packages.' Name the principle and its metric.
questions.explanation: 'Stable = abstract' is SAP, measured by abstractness A = Na/Nc (DesignPrinciplesAndPatterns p.24-26). (DesignPrinciplesAndPatterns p.25)

questions.stem: Given the smell: a function does something AND answers something (e.g. set() returning a boolean used in an if). Name the rule violated.
questions.explanation: A function that both acts and answers violates Command/Query Separation (CleanCode p.32).

questions.stem: Given the smell: PhoneNumber phoneString — the name still says 'String' after the type changed. Name the anti-pattern.
questions.explanation: Encoding the type into the name (phoneString) is Hungarian notation; the name lies when the type changes (CleanCode p.16).

questions.stem: Given the smell: a banner like // Actions /////////////// dividing a file. Name the bad-comment category.
questions.explanation: Banner separators are position-marker comments, a smell (CleanCode p.46).

questions.stem: Given the structure: public static constants, then private statics, then private instance variables, then public functions with helpers after their callers. Name the convention.
questions.explanation: That top-to-bottom ordering is the deck's standard class organization (CleanCode p.78).

questions.stem: A figure (Subject) in a drawing framework notifies its views (Observers) when it changes, without knowing about them. Name the pattern at work.
questions.explanation: A subject notifying views without knowing them is Observer — exactly JHotDraw's Figure->DrawingView case (DesignPrinciplesAndPatterns p.29-30). (DesignPrinciplesAndPatterns p.30)

questions.stem: What is the primary consequence of scattering switch statements on a type code throughout a codebase?

options.text: Unit tests can no longer be written
Each new type forces edits to every switch — a rigidity/OCP problem
The program runs measurably slower at startup

questions.explanation: Scattered type-code switches mean adding a type requires editing every switch, the rigidity/OCP smell (CleanCode p.25-26; DesignPrinciplesAndPatterns p.6). (DesignPrinciplesAndPatterns p.6)

questions.stem: A function takes a boolean flag argument. What does this most directly tell a reader?

options.text: The function returns null on failure
The function is pure and side-effect free
The function violates the Law of Demeter
The function probably does more than one thing

questions.explanation: A flag argument advertises that the function does more than one thing (CleanCode p.29). It says nothing about purity, null, or Demeter. (CleanCode p.29)

questions.stem: An API returns null instead of an empty collection. What consequence does the deck warn about?

options.text: Every caller must add a null check, cluttering call sites

questions.explanation: Null-returning collection APIs force null checks at every call site; returning an empty collection avoids this (CleanCode p.71-72). (CleanCode p.71)

questions.stem: All four rotting-design symptoms (rigidity, fragility, immobility, viscosity) trace back to what single cause?
questions.explanation: Martin attributes all four symptoms directly or indirectly to improper dependencies (DesignPrinciplesAndPatterns p.4).

questions.stem: A function grows to 150 lines doing five jobs. What maintenance consequence does the deck imply?

options.text: It automatically satisfies the Stepdown Rule
It compiles faster than five small functions
It becomes hard to understand, test, and change — it should be split

questions.explanation: Large multi-job functions are hard to understand and change; the rule is small / one thing (CleanCode p.23).

questions.stem: Duplicated logic is left in four places. Why does the deck call duplication 'the root of all evil'?

options.text: It forces the use of flag arguments
A change must be made in every copy, and missing one introduces bugs

questions.explanation: Duplication multiplies the change effort and the chance of inconsistent edits, hence DRY (CleanCode p.33).

questions.stem: Tests are written that are slow and depend on each other's order. What does the deck warn this leads to?

options.text: They run faster than independent tests
They erode trust in the suite, undermining its use as a safety net

questions.explanation: FIRST demands Fast and Independent tests; slow, order-dependent tests undermine the suite (CleanCode p.77).

questions.stem: You want a function to be maximally easy to call and reason about. According to the deck, the best argument count to aim for is:
questions.explanation: 'The ideal number of arguments for a function is zero' (CleanCode p.27).

questions.stem: To make business logic read clearly without special-case catches for a missing meal expense, what is the most appropriate technique?

options.text: Return null and null-check at the caller
Add a flag argument for the special case
Return a Special Case object that already knows the per-diem default

questions.explanation: The Special Case pattern lets the DAO return an object encoding the default so the caller has no special case (CleanCode p.69-70). (CleanCode p.69)

questions.stem: You have several related bare fields firstName, lastName, street, city, state, zipcode. The best fix per the deck is to:

options.text: Encode the type into each name
Convert them to a single magic string
Group them into a class such as Address

questions.explanation: Grouping related fields into a class (Address) is preferred over prefixing each (CleanCode p.21).

questions.stem: To avoid the train wreck a.getB().getC().getD(), the most appropriate refactor per Demeter is to:

options.text: Add a flag argument to skip navigation
Expose the intermediate fields as public
Inline the whole chain on one line
Avoid navigating deep graphs — ask the immediate friend to do the work

questions.explanation: Demeter says talk only to immediate friends rather than navigating deep object graphs (CleanCode p.64). Exposing fields is the worst form. (CleanCode p.64)

questions.stem: The most appropriate response to discovering bad code, per the deck's headline comment rule, is to:

options.text: Mark it with a journal comment
Wrap it in a banner position marker
Rewrite the bad code rather than commenting it
Add a thorough comment explaining the badness

questions.explanation: 'Don't comment bad code — rewrite it' (CleanCode p.34).

questions.stem: A teammate argues 'more comments always make code more maintainable.' Which deck position corrects this?

options.text: Comments should restate each line of code
Every function must have a Javadoc
The default is to explain yourself in code; a comment is often a failure to express intent

questions.explanation: The deck devotes far more slides to bad comments and says 'explain yourself in code' — a comment is a small failure (CleanCode p.34-52). (CleanCode p.34)

questions.stem: A reviewer reads SRP as 'a class should have only one method.' Why is that a misreading?

options.text: SRP means one reason to change, not one method; it is measured in responsibilities
SRP means one field per class

questions.explanation: SRP says a class should have one, and only one, reason to change — about responsibilities, not method count (CleanCode p.79).

questions.stem: A developer says 'a switch statement is always a clean-code violation.' What is the nuance?

options.text: One switch hidden inside a factory creating polymorphic objects is acceptable; scattered type-code switches are the smell
Switches must be replaced by flag arguments
Switches are only bad inside tests

questions.explanation: A single switch quarantined in a factory is acceptable; scattered switches on a type code are the smell (CleanCode p.26; DesignPrinciplesAndPatterns p.6). (CleanCode p.26)

questions.stem: Someone equates Command/Query Separation with the architectural CQRS pattern. What's the distinction in this deck?

options.text: The deck's CQS is the small rule — a function does OR answers, not both — not the CQRS architecture

questions.explanation: The deck's CQS is the function-level do-or-answer rule, not the architectural CQRS (CleanCode p.32).

questions.stem: A teammate thinks 'every public method needs a Javadoc and every private one too.' What does the deck actually say?

options.text: No method should ever have a Javadoc
Public-API Javadoc is good, but Javadoc on nonpublic code adds wasted formality
All methods, public and private, require Javadoc

questions.explanation: Public-API Javadoc is endorsed; Javadoc on internal code is wasted formality (CleanCode p.37, p.52). (CleanCode p.52)

questions.stem: A reviewer insists on aligning all variable declarations into neat columns. What is the deck's actual stance?

options.text: It discourages column alignment; ordinary indentation is enough
It requires column alignment for readability
It mandates tabs over spaces for alignment

questions.explanation: Martin discourages aligning declaration columns; plain indentation suffices (CleanCode p.57-58). (CleanCode p.57)

questions.stem: A developer thinks solution-domain names like AccountVisitor or JobQueue are bad because they're 'too technical.' Why does the deck disagree?

options.text: Readers of your code are programmers, so precise CS terms are acceptable and informative

questions.explanation: Solution-domain names are fine because 'people who read your code will be programmers' (CleanCode p.21).

questions.stem: Which set are the package COHESION principles (which classes go together)?
questions.explanation: Cohesion principles are REP, CCP, CRP; coupling principles are ADP, SDP, SAP (DesignPrinciplesAndPatterns p.16-25). (DesignPrinciplesAndPatterns p.17)

questions.stem: Which set are the package COUPLING principles (relationships between packages)?
questions.explanation: Coupling principles are ADP, SDP, SAP (DesignPrinciplesAndPatterns p.18-25). (DesignPrinciplesAndPatterns p.18)

questions.stem: In the standard class organization, which group comes FIRST from top to bottom?
questions.explanation: Order: public static constants -> private statics -> private instance variables -> public functions (CleanCode p.78).

questions.stem: The Stepdown Rule says a source file should be read in which order?

options.text: Top to bottom, each function followed by those one level of abstraction below it
Bottom to top, lowest-level functions first

questions.explanation: Code reads top to bottom like a narrative, the Stepdown Rule (CleanCode p.24).

questions.stem: JHotDraw's Figure model notifies DrawingView and FigureListeners when a figure changes, without the figure knowing the views. Which pattern is this?
questions.explanation: Figure (Subject) notifying views (Observers) without knowing them is Observer (DesignPrinciplesAndPatterns p.29-30; guide JHotDraw section). (DesignPrinciplesAndPatterns p.30)

questions.stem: JHotDraw creates tools and figures through factory abstractions so the framework never hard-codes concrete figure classes. Which pattern does this illustrate?
questions.explanation: Creating figures via factory abstractions to avoid hard-coding concrete classes is Abstract Factory (DesignPrinciplesAndPatterns p.32; guide JHotDraw section). (DesignPrinciplesAndPatterns p.32)

questions.stem: JHotDraw wraps AWT/Swing components and external types to fit its own interfaces. Which pattern is at work?
questions.explanation: Fitting external/AWT types to JHotDraw's interfaces is the Adapter pattern (DesignPrinciplesAndPatterns p.29; guide JHotDraw section). (DesignPrinciplesAndPatterns p.29)

questions.stem: Why is JHotDraw famous as a teaching example of GoF patterns specifically?

options.text: It was the first program ever written in Java
It is the reference implementation of the SOLID acronym
It contains no patterns, serving as a counterexample
GoF author Erich Gamma co-wrote it to demonstrate patterns in a real framework

questions.explanation: JHotDraw is a textbook GoF showcase because GoF author Erich Gamma co-wrote it (guide JHotDraw section). (DesignPrinciplesAndPatterns p.28)

questions.stem: Read this name: class DtaRcrd102 with field genymdhms. Which naming rule does it most clearly break?
questions.explanation: genymdhms can't be pronounced or discussed; use pronounceable names like generationTimestamp (CleanCode p.13).

questions.stem: Read this fragment: int a = l; if (O == l). Why is it a problem?

options.text: The names l and O are disinformation — indistinguishable from 1 and 0

questions.explanation: Single letters l/O look like 1/0, the disinformation smell (CleanCode p.12).

questions.stem: Read this comment: assertTrue(a.compareTo(b) == -1); // a < b. Which good-comment category is this?
questions.explanation: Translating an obscure expression into readable terms is a clarification comment (CleanCode p.36).

questions.stem: Read this comment on a setter: // The default port is 8082, placed on an unrelated setter. Which bad-comment category?
questions.explanation: A comment describing something far from the code it sits next to is nonlocal information (CleanCode p.49).

questions.stem: Your codebase uses fetch, retrieve, and get for the same operation in different classes. Which naming rule does this break?
questions.explanation: Mixing synonyms for one concept breaks 'pick one word per concept' (CleanCode p.20).

questions.stem: You use add() for numeric addition in one place and for list insertion in another. Which rule does this break?

options.text: Don't pun (don't use one word for two purposes)

questions.explanation: Using add for two different purposes is a pun, which the deck forbids (CleanCode p.20).

questions.stem: Where should a local variable be declared, per the vertical-distance rule?

options.text: As close to its usage as possible
Always at the bottom of the method
Always at the top of the file

questions.explanation: Declare variables as close to their usage as possible (CleanCode p.55).

questions.stem: Where should instance variables be declared, per the deck?

options.text: Inside the first method that uses them
At the top of the class
At the very bottom of the class

questions.explanation: Instance variables belong at the top of the class (CleanCode p.55).

questions.stem: Two functions call each other (caller and callee). Where should they sit vertically?

options.text: Close together, with the caller above the callee

questions.explanation: Dependent functions should be vertically close, caller above callee (CleanCode p.55).

questions.stem: When a team's formatting convention conflicts with your personal preference, which wins?
questions.explanation: 'If he works in a team then the team rules' win (CleanCode p.60).

questions.stem: In F.I.R.S.T., what does 'Timely' mean?

options.text: Tests are written long after the code ships
Tests are written just before the production code they cover
Tests run at a scheduled time of day

questions.explanation: Timely means writing tests just before the production code (CleanCode p.77).

questions.stem: What payoff is the 'one assert per test' guideline reaching for?

options.text: Each test reaches a single, quick-to-understand conclusion

questions.explanation: One assert per test yields a single clear conclusion (CleanCode p.76).

questions.stem: How does the deck say class size should be measured?
questions.explanation: Classes should be small, measured in responsibilities (CleanCode p.79).

questions.stem: What does maintaining high cohesion tend to produce, per the deck?
questions.explanation: Maintaining cohesion results in many small classes (CleanCode p.79).

questions.stem: A comment reads '// This is our best attempt to get a race condition by creating many threads.' Which good-comment category?
questions.explanation: Explaining the why behind code is an intent comment (CleanCode p.36).

questions.stem: A comment reads '// format matched kk:mm:ss EEE, MMM dd, yyyy' above a regex. Which good-comment category?
questions.explanation: Documenting the format a regex matches is an informative comment (CleanCode p.35).

questions.stem: A comment insists a .trim() call 'is real important - it removes the starting spaces.' Into which good-comment category does it fall?
questions.explanation: Stressing something easy to overlook is an amplification comment (CleanCode p.37).

questions.stem: A catch block carries only '// No properties files means all defaults are loaded,' so you must read elsewhere to follow it. Which bad-comment category is that?
questions.explanation: A terse comment that forces reading other code to understand it is mumbling (CleanCode p.38).

questions.stem: A Javadoc restates the code and is longer than what it documents, adding nothing. Which bad-comment category?
questions.explanation: A comment that merely restates the code is redundant (CleanCode p.39).

questions.stem: Policy mandates Javadoc on every single function and variable, and the result is clutter. Which bad-comment category does this produce?
questions.explanation: Policy-forced Javadoc everywhere is the mandated-comments smell (CleanCode p.41).

questions.stem: Source code carries an embedded 'Changes (from 11-Oct-2001) ...' history. Which bad-comment category is it, and where does that history belong instead?

options.text: Journal comment — it belongs in version control
Legal comment — it belongs in a license file
Warning comment — it belongs in a README

questions.explanation: Embedded change logs are journal comments; version control should hold them (CleanCode p.42).

questions.stem: Comments like '/** Default constructor. */' and '/** The day of the month. */' fall under which bad-comment category?
questions.explanation: Comments that say nothing are noise (CleanCode p.43).

questions.stem: Instead of writing a comment to explain a complex boolean condition, the deck recommends:

options.text: Move the condition into a journal comment
Add a banner comment above it
Leave it commented out as a backup
Extract the condition into a well-named variable or function

questions.explanation: Use a function or variable instead of a comment for a complex condition (CleanCode p.45).

questions.stem: A '/* Added by Rick */' line is which bad-comment category, and which tool already records that information?

options.text: A legal comment — redundant with a license
A warning comment — redundant with tests
An informative comment — redundant with a README
An attribution/byline — redundant with version control

questions.explanation: Attributions/bylines are made redundant by version control (CleanCode p.47).

questions.stem: Pasting a whole RFC excerpt into a source comment is an instance of which bad-comment category?
questions.explanation: Dumping excessive external text into a comment is too-much-information (CleanCode p.50).

questions.stem: The '200 bytes for header info' comment leaves its link to the code unclear. Which bad-comment category does that match?
questions.explanation: A comment with an unclear link to its code is the inobvious-connection smell (CleanCode p.51).

questions.stem: You are cleaning a method that returns -1 to signal failure, forcing callers to test the result. Which TWO Clean Code rules push you toward throwing an exception instead?

options.text: Don't pass null, and use searchable names
Prefer exceptions to error codes, and define the normal flow
One level of abstraction, and the Stepdown Rule

questions.explanation: A magic -1 error code is exactly what 'prefer exceptions to error codes' (CleanCode p.65) and 'define the normal flow' (CleanCode p.69) target. The other pairs concern nulls, names, dual-purpose methods, and abstraction levels. (CleanCode p.65)

questions.stem: You inherit a function whose first line is setup code, followed by a try/catch, followed by cleanup code after the catch. Applying 'error handling is one thing,' what do you change?

options.text: Make try the first word and have nothing after the catch/finally
Add a flag argument to toggle error handling
Replace the try/catch with an error-code return
Move the catch body into the try body

questions.explanation: 'Error handling is one thing' means if try appears it must be the very first word, with nothing after the catch/finally (CleanCode p.68). Error codes, merging bodies, and flags all contradict the rule. (CleanCode p.68)

questions.stem: You are cleaning a reporting method that has a try/catch around five lines of business logic. To separate concerns, what does Clean Code say to do with the try and catch bodies?

options.text: Add a comment explaining each branch
Extract each into a one-line call to its own method
Replace the try/catch with nested if (x==E_OK) checks

questions.explanation: 'Extract try/catch blocks' says the bodies should be one-line calls to extracted methods so error handling stays separate from logic (CleanCode p.67). Inlining, error codes, and comments do not separate the concerns. (CleanCode p.67)

questions.stem: A collection-returning API you maintain returns null when empty, and every caller writes if (list != null). To clean this up, you should:

options.text: Return Collections.emptyList() so callers can iterate unconditionally
Pass a flag telling the method to skip the null
Return null but document it in Javadoc

questions.explanation: 'Don't return null' from a collection API; return an empty collection so callers drop their null checks (CleanCode p.71-72). Throwing, documenting, or flagging keeps the burden on callers. (CleanCode p.71)

questions.stem: You see private String m_dsc with setName assigning m_dsc. As you clean this, which two changes does the deck imply?

options.text: Add Hungarian type encoding to the name
Convert the field to an error code
Drop the m_ prefix and rename to a clear field like description
Keep m_ but add a Javadoc

questions.explanation: Member prefixes like m_ are redundant; rename to a clear field such as description (CleanCode p.15). Hungarian encoding is itself an anti-pattern, and Javadoc/error codes don't address the prefix. (CleanCode p.15)

questions.stem: You are cleaning a function makeCircle(double x, double y, double radius) and want fewer arguments. What is the deck's technique?

options.text: Return the circle via an out-parameter
Merge x and y into a single magic number
Introduce an argument object: makeCircle(Point center, double radius)
Add a flag to select the coordinate system

questions.explanation: When functions need many related arguments, wrap them in an object: makeCircle(Point center, double radius) (CleanCode p.31). Flags, out-parameters, and magic numbers add smells rather than removing them. (CleanCode p.31)

questions.stem: Which of the following would NOT count as a violation of 'do one thing' for a function named isAuthenticated()?

options.text: It only evaluates and returns whether the user is authenticated
It also writes an audit log entry
It also sends a notification email
It also updates a failed-attempt counter

questions.explanation: A query that only computes and returns its answer does one thing and respects Command/Query Separation (CleanCode p.32). Logging, mutating a counter, or emailing add side effects. (CleanCode p.32)

questions.stem: Which is NOT a correct description of the stability metric I = Ce/(Ca+Ce)?
questions.explanation: I=0 is maximally stable; I=1 is maximally instable (DesignPrinciplesAndPatterns p.23-24). Saying I=1 is stable inverts the scale. (DesignPrinciplesAndPatterns p.23)

questions.stem: Which is NOT a way Martin describes for breaking a package dependency cycle?

options.text: Create a new package to absorb the shared dependency
Place the inverting interface in the package that uses it
Merge the two packages into one large package
Apply DIP+ISP to invert one dependency via an interface

questions.explanation: ADP cycle-breaking uses (1) a new package or (2) DIP+ISP inversion with the interface in the using package (DesignPrinciplesAndPatterns p.20-22). Merging packages is not the prescribed remedy. (DesignPrinciplesAndPatterns p.20)

questions.stem: Which is NOT true of the Liskov Substitution Principle as restated by Design by Contract?

options.text: A derived method's preconditions must be no stronger than the base's
Derived methods should expect no more and provide no less
A derived method's postconditions must be no weaker than the base's
A derived method may strengthen the base method's preconditions

questions.explanation: Substitutability requires preconditions no stronger and postconditions no weaker; strengthening a precondition breaks LSP (DesignPrinciplesAndPatterns p.11).

questions.stem: Both Adapter and Abstract Server insert an abstract interface between client and server. What distinguishes when you reach for Adapter?

options.text: The server cannot be modified (third-party or over-depended-upon)
The interface must be a concrete class
The server has no methods to delegate to
The client and server are in the same package

questions.explanation: Abstract Server is used when you CAN change the server; Adapter is for when you CAN'T, delegating to an unmodifiable server (DesignPrinciplesAndPatterns p.28-29). (DesignPrinciplesAndPatterns p.29)

questions.stem: Set Bridge against Adapter: how do their intents diverge?

options.text: Bridge is creational; Adapter is behavioral
Bridge wraps a third-party server; Adapter creates objects
Bridge separates an abstraction from its implementation so both vary independently; Adapter wraps an existing incompatible server
Bridge notifies observers; Adapter registers them

questions.explanation: Bridge decouples abstraction from implementation up front so each can vary; Adapter retrofits an interface onto an existing/unchangeable server (DesignPrinciplesAndPatterns p.29-31). (DesignPrinciplesAndPatterns p.30)

questions.stem: Rigidity and fragility are often confused. Which contrast is correct?

options.text: Rigidity = can't reuse a module; fragility = slow builds
Both describe the inability to reuse code elsewhere
Rigidity = breaks in unrelated places; fragility = hard to change
Rigidity = hard to change (cascades); fragility = breaks unexpectedly, often in unrelated areas, when changed

questions.explanation: Rigidity is the cost of making changes (cascades); fragility is breakage in many, often unrelated places after a change (DesignPrinciplesAndPatterns p.2). The other options describe immobility/viscosity or swap the two. (DesignPrinciplesAndPatterns p.2)

questions.stem: Observer and Abstract Server both reduce coupling. What is the distinctive intent of Observer?

options.text: Wrap an unmodifiable server behind an interface
Quarantine concrete-class creation in one place
Let a detector notify actors of an event without knowing who they are
Separate an abstraction from its implementation

questions.explanation: Observer lets a Subject notify registered Observers without the detector knowing the actors (DesignPrinciplesAndPatterns p.29). Wrapping, creation, and abstraction/implementation are Adapter, Abstract Factory, and Bridge. (DesignPrinciplesAndPatterns p.29)

questions.stem: Compare CCP and CRP. Which captures their opposing pulls on package size?

options.text: CCP groups classes that change together (larger packages for maintainers); CRP separates classes not reused together (smaller packages for reusers)
Both push toward maximally large packages
CCP serves reusers; CRP serves maintainers
Both push toward maximally small packages

questions.explanation: CCP (change together -> belong together) favours maintainers and larger packages; CRP (not reused together -> don't group) favours reusers and smaller packages (DesignPrinciplesAndPatterns p.17-18). (DesignPrinciplesAndPatterns p.18)

questions.stem: Abstract Factory and Abstract Server both involve an abstract interface. What problem is unique to Abstract Factory?

options.text: Object creation forces a concrete dependency, which it confines to one place
An abstraction is too tightly coupled to its implementation
A detector must notify actors anonymously
A client cannot modify a third-party server

questions.explanation: Abstract Factory addresses the DIP gap that creating an instance names a concrete class; it quarantines that knowledge in one place (DesignPrinciplesAndPatterns p.32). The others are Adapter, Observer, and Bridge concerns. (DesignPrinciplesAndPatterns p.32)

questions.stem: How do immobility and viscosity differ as rotting-design symptoms?

options.text: Immobility = a module can't be reused due to dependency baggage; viscosity = the design-preserving change is harder than the hack
They are two names for the same symptom
Immobility = code breaks unexpectedly; viscosity = code can't be reused
Immobility = builds are slow; viscosity = changes cascade

questions.explanation: Immobility is inability to reuse because of dependency baggage; viscosity is when doing the right (design-preserving) thing is harder than the hack (DesignPrinciplesAndPatterns p.3).

questions.stem: CCP is best understood as the package-level analogue of which class-level principle?
questions.explanation: CCP groups classes that change for the same reasons, making it SRP for packages (DesignPrinciplesAndPatterns p.17).

questions.stem: A client depends on a concrete logging server you OWN, and later must also support a third-party logger you CANNOT change. Which two patterns address these, in order?
questions.explanation: Insert an Abstract Server interface when you can change the server; use an Adapter when you cannot modify the third-party one (DesignPrinciplesAndPatterns p.28-29). (DesignPrinciplesAndPatterns p.29)

questions.stem: Compare REP and CRP. What do they have in common, and how does this set them against CCP?

options.text: All three favour the same package size
Both serve reusers and favour smaller packages, opposing CCP which serves maintainers with larger ones
Both serve maintainers, opposing CCP which serves reusers
REP and CRP are coupling principles; CCP is cohesion

questions.explanation: REP and CRP serve reusers (smaller packages); CCP serves maintainers (larger packages), creating the cohesion tension (DesignPrinciplesAndPatterns p.18). All three are cohesion principles. (DesignPrinciplesAndPatterns p.18)

questions.stem: A package CommError accidentally gains a dependency on GUI, forming a cycle. What is the dangerous consequence under ADP?

options.text: The whole transitive closure can be pulled into CommError's build and test
CommError's instability metric drops to zero

questions.explanation: A single cyclic dependency can pull a package's entire transitive closure into its build/test, increasing it 'by an abhorrent amount' (DesignPrinciplesAndPatterns p.18-20). (DesignPrinciplesAndPatterns p.20)

questions.stem: A method navigates a.getB().getC().getD() through several objects. In change-process terms, what does this Law-of-Demeter violation cause?

options.text: It removes the need for unit tests
It guarantees the method is independent for FIRST
It makes the method run in constant time
It enlarges the estimated impact set — a change to any traversed type ripples to this caller

questions.explanation: Deep navigation couples the caller to every traversed type, so changes ripple and the impact set grows (CleanCode p.64).

questions.stem: A high-level policy module depends directly on a volatile concrete detail class. Per DIP, what is the consequence?

options.text: The policy gains a lower instability metric
The detail class becomes abstract automatically
The stable policy is dragged along whenever the volatile detail changes
The dependency forms an acyclic graph by default

questions.explanation: Depending on concretions couples stable policy to volatile detail; DIP inverts this so policy depends on an abstraction (DesignPrinciplesAndPatterns p.12-13). (DesignPrinciplesAndPatterns p.13)

questions.stem: A fat interface is shared by clients A, B, and C. A change requested by client A forces what?

options.text: A cycle in the package dependency graph
An increase in the abstractness metric
Recompilation of clients B and C even though they didn't need the change

questions.explanation: A fat interface couples its clients, so a change for one forces recompilation of the others — the ISP problem (DesignPrinciplesAndPatterns p.15).

questions.stem: A reviewer leaves '} //while' closing-brace comments everywhere. What does the deck say this signals, and the consequence?

options.text: Blocks are too long; shorten the function instead of annotating braces
The function is too short to read
The code is well documented and needs no changes

questions.explanation: Closing-brace comments signal blocks that are too big; the cure is shorter functions (CleanCode p.46).

questions.stem: Your client depends on a server class you control, and you want to be able to swap implementations later without touching the client. Which pattern is the most appropriate first move?

options.text: Adapter — wrap the server by delegation
Observer — register the client as a listener
Abstract Server — insert an interface as a hinge point

questions.explanation: When you can modify the server, inserting an Abstract Server interface gives the swap-ability (DesignPrinciplesAndPatterns p.28). Adapter is for unmodifiable servers. (DesignPrinciplesAndPatterns p.28)

questions.stem: You must integrate a closed-source third-party library whose API doesn't match your interface and which you cannot modify. Best pattern?
questions.explanation: When the server can't be changed, an Adapter implements your interface and delegates to it (DesignPrinciplesAndPatterns p.29).

questions.stem: Several display widgets must update whenever a data source changes, but the data source must not know about the widgets. Best pattern?
questions.explanation: Decoupling an event source from anonymous consumers is exactly Observer (DesignPrinciplesAndPatterns p.29).

questions.stem: You need to vary both a rendering abstraction and several rendering implementations independently, without combinatorial subclassing. Best pattern?
questions.explanation: Bridge separates abstraction from implementation so each can vary independently (DesignPrinciplesAndPatterns p.30-31). (DesignPrinciplesAndPatterns p.31)

questions.stem: You want no high-level module to know any concrete product class, confining that knowledge to a single creation point. Best pattern?
questions.explanation: Abstract Factory quarantines concrete-class creation in one place (DesignPrinciplesAndPatterns p.32).

questions.stem: A unit test currently asserts six unrelated things. The best clean-test adjustment is to:

options.text: Split it so each test covers a single concept with minimal asserts
Remove all asserts and rely on no exceptions
Combine the asserts into one giant assertTrue
Add a flag argument to select which assert runs

questions.explanation: Tests should test just one concept and minimize asserts per concept (CleanCode p.76).

questions.stem: You want a stable, heavily depended-upon package to remain easy to extend despite being hard to modify. The most appropriate move is to:

options.text: Merge it with an instable package

questions.explanation: SAP says stable packages should be abstract so they extend without modification (DesignPrinciplesAndPatterns p.24-25). (DesignPrinciplesAndPatterns p.24)

questions.stem: You discover a cycle between two packages. Per Martin, the most appropriate FIRST design move is often to:

options.text: Delete one of the packages entirely
Create a new package to absorb the shared dependency
Add more cycles to balance the graph

questions.explanation: One of the two ADP cycle-breaking tactics is to create a new package; the other is DIP+ISP inversion (DesignPrinciplesAndPatterns p.20-22). (DesignPrinciplesAndPatterns p.20)

questions.stem: You must create modems polymorphically while keeping every high-level module ignorant of the concrete classes. The most appropriate pattern, and the DIP gap it closes, is:

options.text: Bridge — it separates the modem interface from its implementation
Adapter — it wraps an unmodifiable modem
Abstract Factory — it confines the unavoidable concrete dependency of object creation to one place
Observer — it notifies modems of events

questions.explanation: Abstract Factory resolves the DIP gap that creation names a concrete class, confining it to one place (DesignPrinciplesAndPatterns p.32).

questions.stem: A developer says 'stable means good and instable means bad, so we should make every package stable.' What is the misconception?

options.text: All packages must sit in the Zone of Pain
Stability is measured by A = Na/Nc
Instability is desirable for packages you want to keep changeable; SDP only forbids stable->instable dependencies

questions.explanation: Instability is desirable where change is expected; SDP just says don't depend in the unstable direction (DesignPrinciplesAndPatterns p.24).

questions.stem: Someone claims 'Circle is-a Ellipse mathematically, so inheriting Circle from Ellipse is obviously correct.' What's wrong?

options.text: Inheritance models behavioral substitutability, not is-a taxonomy; the Ellipse contract breaks for Circle
Circle and Ellipse are unrelated shapes
Ellipse should inherit from Circle instead

questions.explanation: LSP is about behavioral substitutability; Circle overriding SetFoci breaks the Ellipse contract clients rely on (DesignPrinciplesAndPatterns p.9-11). (DesignPrinciplesAndPatterns p.10)

questions.stem: A developer thinks 'returning an empty collection is the same as returning null.' Why is this wrong?

options.text: An empty collection lets callers iterate safely; null forces a check and risks NullPointerException
They are indeed identical in Java
Null collections are faster to allocate

questions.explanation: Returning an empty collection avoids the null checks that null forces; 'don't return null' (CleanCode p.71-72). (CleanCode p.71)

questions.stem: A developer believes depending on any concrete class always violates DIP. What mitigating force does Martin allow?

options.text: Only abstract factories may be depended upon
Stable concretions (e.g. string.h / standard library) are acceptable to depend upon
Depending on abstractions also violates DIP

questions.explanation: DIP assumes concrete = volatile, but stable concretions like string.h are fine to depend on (DesignPrinciplesAndPatterns p.14).

questions.stem: A developer believes 'ISP means create as many tiny interfaces as possible.' What caution does Martin add?

options.text: Don't over-segregate into hundreds of interfaces; segregate by client type
ISP requires exactly one interface per method
ISP and DIP are the same rule

questions.explanation: ISP segregates by client type but warns against over-splitting into hundreds of interfaces (DesignPrinciplesAndPatterns p.16).

questions.stem: Place the four Rules of Simple Design in their correct priority order.

options.text: No duplication; minimal classes/methods; expressive; runs all tests
Runs all tests; no duplication; expressive; minimal classes/methods
Expressive; runs all tests; minimal classes/methods; no duplication
Minimal classes/methods; expressive; no duplication; runs all tests

questions.explanation: Beck's priority order is: runs all tests, no duplication, expressive, minimal classes/methods (CleanCode p.80).

questions.stem: Order the function-argument-count preference from most to least desirable per the deck.
questions.explanation: Fewer is better: zero, then one, then two, then three (CleanCode p.27-30). (CleanCode p.27)

questions.stem: Put the Observer dynamics in order when a Sensor's reading changes.

options.text: Meter calls Update -> Sensor reads -> Subject registers -> displays
Sensor detects change -> calls Notify on Subject -> Subject cycles Observers calling Update -> each Meter reads and displays
Observer notifies Subject -> Sensor updates -> Meter detects change
Subject reads value -> Sensor displays -> Meter notifies -> Observers register

questions.explanation: The flow is detect -> Notify -> Subject cycles Observers calling Update -> Meter reads/displays (DesignPrinciplesAndPatterns p.29-30). (DesignPrinciplesAndPatterns p.30)

questions.stem: Among OCP, LSP, DIP, ISP and SRP, which one is supplied by the Clean Code deck rather than the principles deck?
questions.explanation: The principles deck covers OCP/LSP/DIP/ISP; SRP is stated in the Clean Code deck (CleanCode p.79).

questions.stem: Extending JHotDraw with a new Figure subclass without touching the framework is held up as OCP. What about it makes it OCP?

options.text: The framework is extended by new code while existing code stays closed to modification
It demonstrates ADP because no cycles form
It demonstrates LSP because Figure is abstract
It demonstrates ISP because Figure has many interfaces

questions.explanation: Extending behaviour by adding a subclass without modifying the framework is the OCP payoff (DesignPrinciplesAndPatterns p.4; guide JHotDraw section). (DesignPrinciplesAndPatterns p.4)

questions.stem: Why does the course use JHotDraw to teach Concept Location and Impact Analysis specifically?

options.text: It returns null everywhere as a cautionary example
Its interface-based, single-responsibility design keeps impact sets small and dependencies pointed at abstractions
It has no design patterns to distract students
It deliberately violates the Law of Demeter for teaching

questions.explanation: Clean, pattern-rich, interface-based code keeps Concept Location and Impact Analysis tractable (guide JHotDraw section; DesignPrinciplesAndPatterns p.12). (DesignPrinciplesAndPatterns p.12)

questions.stem: JHotDraw uses Bridge-style separation so figure behavior can vary independently of figure structure. Which related GoF patterns does the guide list alongside Bridge for this?
questions.explanation: The guide pairs Bridge-style separation with Strategy and Composite in JHotDraw (guide JHotDraw section; DesignPrinciplesAndPatterns p.30-31). (DesignPrinciplesAndPatterns p.30)

questions.stem: In a JHotDraw change (Concept Location -> Impact Analysis -> Actualization -> Postfactoring), why does the estimated impact set stay small?

options.text: Because Impact Analysis is skipped on JHotDraw
Because the framework obeys clean-code and OO principles, so dependencies point at abstractions and changes don't cascade
Because JHotDraw has no dependencies at all
Because every change is a hack that ignores design

questions.explanation: The impact set stays small precisely because JHotDraw obeys these principles (guide JHotDraw section). (DesignPrinciplesAndPatterns p.12)

questions.stem: A package has Ca=2 incoming and Ce=8 outgoing dependencies. Compute I = Ce/(Ca+Ce) and classify it.
questions.explanation: I = 8/(2+8) = 0.8, near the instable end (DesignPrinciplesAndPatterns p.23-24). (DesignPrinciplesAndPatterns p.23)

questions.stem: A package has Ca=8 incoming and Ce=2 outgoing dependencies. Compute I and classify it.
questions.explanation: I = 2/(8+2) = 0.2, near the stable end (many depend on it) (DesignPrinciplesAndPatterns p.23-24). (DesignPrinciplesAndPatterns p.23)

questions.stem: A package measures Ca=0 and Ce=5. Compute its instability I, and state what SDP then concludes about it.

options.text: I = 0.5 — it is on the Main Sequence
I = 1 (maximally instable) — it should not be depended upon by more stable packages
I = 0 (maximally stable) — others should depend on it

questions.explanation: I = 5/(0+5) = 1, maximally instable; SDP says depend toward more stable packages, not on this one (DesignPrinciplesAndPatterns p.23-24). (DesignPrinciplesAndPatterns p.23)

questions.stem: A package contains 3 abstract classes out of 12 total. Compute A = Na/Nc.
questions.explanation: A = Na/Nc = 3/12 = 0.25 (DesignPrinciplesAndPatterns p.25-26). (DesignPrinciplesAndPatterns p.25)

questions.stem: A package sits exactly on the Main Sequence with A=0.3. What must its instability I be, and what is D'?
questions.explanation: The Main Sequence is A+I=1, so I = 1-0.3 = 0.7 and D' = 0 (DesignPrinciplesAndPatterns p.26-27). (DesignPrinciplesAndPatterns p.26)

questions.stem: Two packages: P with I=0.2 and Q with I=0.8. Per SDP, which dependency direction is allowed?

options.text: P may depend on Q (stable depends on instable)
Q may depend on P (instable depends on stable)
Neither may depend on the other

questions.explanation: SDP: depend on packages whose I is lower than yours, so Q (0.8) -> P (0.2) is allowed (DesignPrinciplesAndPatterns p.23-24). (DesignPrinciplesAndPatterns p.23)

questions.stem: Read this: abstract class EmployeeType { abstract int payAmount(Employee e); } with Salesman and Manager overrides. Which clean-code/OO move does it implement?

options.text: Replacing a type-code switch with polymorphism (OCP)

questions.explanation: An abstract base with overrides replaces the switch on EmployeeType with polymorphism, the OCP move (CleanCode p.25-26; DesignPrinciplesAndPatterns p.5-7). (CleanCode p.26)

questions.stem: Read this: a Point interface exposing getX()/getY()/setCartesian()/getR()/getTheta()/setPolar(). What does it achieve over a class with public double x, y?

options.text: Data abstraction — it hides whether the point is stored in Cartesian or polar form
It removes the need for unit tests

questions.explanation: The abstract Point hides its representation (Cartesian vs polar) — data abstraction (CleanCode p.61).

questions.stem: Read this: template<typename MODEM> void LogOn(MODEM& m, ...). Which OCP mechanism does it use?
questions.explanation: Templates achieve OCP via static polymorphism (DesignPrinciplesAndPatterns p.7).

questions.stem: Why might 'conceptual affinity' justify placing two functions close even without a direct call between them?

options.text: Related code near related code aids readability even absent a call relationship
It is required by the Three Laws of TDD

questions.explanation: Conceptual affinity keeps related code together for readability (CleanCode p.55).

questions.stem: A copy-pasted noise comment is subtly wrong, e.g. '/** The version. */ private String info;'. What does the deck call this?
questions.explanation: Copy-pasted noise that is subtly wrong is scary noise (CleanCode p.44).

questions.stem: Martin notes 'concrete things change a lot, abstract things change less.' How does that observation back DIP?

options.text: Depending on stable abstractions insulates you from volatile concrete details

questions.explanation: Because concretions are volatile and abstractions stable, depending on abstractions is safer — the DIP rationale (DesignPrinciplesAndPatterns p.13).

questions.stem: LSP is most closely related to which idea by Bertrand Meyer?
questions.explanation: LSP is formalized via Meyer's Design by Contract (DesignPrinciplesAndPatterns p.8, p.11). (DesignPrinciplesAndPatterns p.11)

questions.stem: Why is object creation the place DIP is hardest to honor, and what resolves it?

options.text: Creation needs flag arguments; CQS resolves it
Creation requires null; the Special Case pattern resolves it
Creating an instance names a concrete class; Abstract Factory confines that to one place
Creation is impossible without cycles; ADP resolves it

questions.explanation: Creation forces a concrete dependency; Abstract Factory confines it (DesignPrinciplesAndPatterns p.14, p.32). (DesignPrinciplesAndPatterns p.32)

questions.stem: Walking through the Sensor/Meter Observer setup, by what mechanism does a Meter get registered for a Subject's updates?
questions.explanation: Observers register via Register(Observer*) on the Subject (DesignPrinciplesAndPatterns p.30).

questions.stem: What coupling defect in the MusicSynthesizer example is the Bridge pattern brought in to cure?

options.text: EmitVoice is inextricably bound to PlayMidi via inheritance, so neither can be reused or varied alone
EmitVoice notifies observers it shouldn't know
The synthesizer creates concrete classes directly

questions.explanation: Inheritance binds EmitVoice to PlayMidi so they can't vary independently — Bridge separates them (DesignPrinciplesAndPatterns p.31).

questions.stem: In the modem Abstract Factory example, knowledge of the concrete Hayes/Courrier/Ernie classes is kept in just one place. Where?

options.text: In every client that calls Make
In the concrete ModemFactory_I, created only by main

questions.explanation: Only ModemFactory_I knows the concrete classes, and only main knows ModemFactory_I (DesignPrinciplesAndPatterns p.32).

questions.stem: Your design has a stable package depending on an instable one. Why does SDP flag this?

options.text: It is always acceptable if both are tested
Changes to the instable package will force changes to the supposedly stable one
It raises the abstractness of both
It creates an acyclic graph automatically

questions.explanation: SDP: depend toward stability; a stable package depending on an instable one inherits its volatility (DesignPrinciplesAndPatterns p.23-24). (DesignPrinciplesAndPatterns p.23)

questions.stem: Why is it dangerous, under ADP, for CommError to depend on GUI?

options.text: A single such dependency can pull GUI's whole transitive closure into CommError's build and test

questions.explanation: One cyclic edge can drag a whole transitive closure into the build/test (DesignPrinciplesAndPatterns p.18-20). (DesignPrinciplesAndPatterns p.20)

questions.stem: A developer adds a flag argument to avoid splitting a function, arguing 'it's just one boolean.' What does the deck say?

options.text: Booleans should be replaced by error codes
A flag argument is a smell because it announces the function does more than one thing — split it

questions.explanation: Even one flag argument signals multiple responsibilities; split into two functions (CleanCode p.29).

questions.stem: A reviewer says 'ISP just means small classes.' Correct them.

options.text: ISP is about small, client-specific interfaces, not class size

questions.explanation: ISP concerns segregating interfaces by client type, distinct from class size (DesignPrinciplesAndPatterns p.14-16). (DesignPrinciplesAndPatterns p.14)

questions.stem: Of the Rules of Simple Design, which ranks first in priority, and on what grounds?

options.text: Minimal classes/methods — fewer is always better
Runs all the tests — a design that can't be verified isn't done

questions.explanation: 'Runs all the tests' is first; an unverifiable design isn't finished (CleanCode p.80).

questions.stem: How does the Emergence slide bridge micro (clean code) to macro (good design)?

options.text: It mandates one giant class per system
It abandons clean code for big up-front design
Applying small rules relentlessly lets good architecture emerge

questions.explanation: Emergence: apply small rules and architecture takes care of itself (CleanCode p.80).

questions.stem: SAP is described as a package-scale restatement of which class-level principle, and why?

options.text: ISP — packages should expose client-specific interfaces
DIP — stable (depended-upon) packages should be abstract so they can be extended without modification
SRP — packages should change for one reason

questions.explanation: SAP says stable packages should be abstract, which is 'just a restatement of the DIP' at package scale (DesignPrinciplesAndPatterns p.24-25). (DesignPrinciplesAndPatterns p.24)

questions.stem: A subtype strengthens a base method's precondition (accepts fewer inputs). What is the downstream consequence for callers of the base type?

options.text: The base class gains a new postcondition
Existing callers that relied on the base contract may now fail — an LSP violation

questions.explanation: Strengthening preconditions breaks substitutability; callers built against the base contract can fail (DesignPrinciplesAndPatterns p.11).

questions.stem: A team places a concrete, heavily depended-upon package in the Zone of Pain. What practical trouble follows?

options.text: Nobody depends on it, so it is dead code
It can't be extended (concrete) yet is painful to change (many depend on it)
Its instability metric I rises toward 1
It automatically lands on the Main Sequence
