questions.explanation: The Zone of Pain (low A, low I) is concrete and heavily depended-upon: hard to change, can't extend (DesignPrinciplesAndPatterns p.27).

questions.stem: A team brags it has simultaneously maximized REP, CCP, and CRP. Why is the claim suspect?

options.text: The three cohesion principles are mutually exclusive and cannot all be maximized at once
REP and CRP are coupling principles, not cohesion
CCP is not a real principle
They forgot to also maximize ADP

questions.explanation: REP, CCP, and CRP are in tension and cannot be simultaneously satisfied (DesignPrinciplesAndPatterns p.18).

questions.stem: A developer says 'LSP and OCP are unrelated concerns.' What connection does the deck draw?

options.text: LSP violations are latent OCP violations — the type-check fixes they invite must change for every new subtype
OCP is a special case of ISP

questions.explanation: The deck states LSP violations are latent OCP violations because the defensive type checks they cause must be revisited per subtype (DesignPrinciplesAndPatterns p.11-12). (DesignPrinciplesAndPatterns p.12)

questions.stem: Given the cohesion tension, how should a package's structure evolve over a project's life?

options.text: Fixed at project start and never changed
Always maximally REP from day one
CCP-dominant early (maintainability), shifting toward REP/CRP as it stabilizes (reusability)
REP/CRP-dominant early, shifting to CCP late

questions.explanation: The structure should jitter: CCP-dominant early, REP/CRP-dominant as it stabilizes (DesignPrinciplesAndPatterns p.18).

questions.stem: On the A-vs-I graph, which corner is the Zone of Pain and which is the Zone of Uselessness?

options.text: Zone of Pain = low A / low I (lower-left); Zone of Uselessness = high A / high I (upper-right)
Zone of Pain = high A / low I; Zone of Uselessness = low A / high I
Zone of Pain = high A / high I; Zone of Uselessness = low A / low I
Both are on the Main Sequence

questions.explanation: Zone of Pain is lower-left (concrete, depended-upon); Zone of Uselessness is upper-right (abstract, unused) (DesignPrinciplesAndPatterns p.27).

questions.stem: If JHotDraw violated the Law of Demeter, returned nulls, and switched on type codes, which symptoms would it exhibit?

options.text: It would land exactly on the Main Sequence
Rigidity and fragility — every maintenance task would become expensive

questions.explanation: Those violations produce the rigidity/fragility symptoms that make maintenance costly (guide JHotDraw section; DesignPrinciplesAndPatterns p.2-3). (DesignPrinciplesAndPatterns p.2)

questions.stem: Which combination best explains JHotDraw's value to this maintenance course?

options.text: Pattern-rich + interface-based (DIP) + small SRP classes makes Concept Location and Impact Analysis cheap
Monolithic + concrete-class dependencies makes refactoring unnecessary
No tests + many nulls makes Verification trivial
Heavy Demeter violations make impact sets predictable

questions.explanation: Its pattern-rich, DIP/SRP design is why the course uses it — it keeps Concept Location and Impact Analysis tractable (guide JHotDraw section; CleanCode p.79). (DesignPrinciplesAndPatterns p.12)

questions.stem: A package sits at A=0.9, I=0.9. Where is it and what is its distance D' = |A+I-1|?

options.text: Zone of Uselessness; D' = 0.8 (far from the Main Sequence)

questions.explanation: A+I = 1.8 so D' = |1.8-1| = 0.8; high A and high I is the Zone of Uselessness (DesignPrinciplesAndPatterns p.26-27). (DesignPrinciplesAndPatterns p.27)

questions.stem: A package sits at A=0.05, I=0.05. Where is it and what is D'?
questions.explanation: A+I = 0.1 so D' = |0.1-1| = 0.9; low A and low I is the Zone of Pain (DesignPrinciplesAndPatterns p.26-27). (DesignPrinciplesAndPatterns p.27)

questions.stem: Package P is abstract (A=0.9) and heavily depended-upon (I=0.1). How should you judge it?

options.text: Off any axis — the metrics don't apply
Healthy — abstract-and-stable, near the upper-left of the Main Sequence
Unhealthy — it is in the Zone of Uselessness
Unhealthy — it is in the Zone of Pain

questions.explanation: A+I = 1.0, so it sits on the Main Sequence at the abstract-stable end — the ideal for a depended-upon package (DesignPrinciplesAndPatterns p.26-27). (DesignPrinciplesAndPatterns p.26)

questions.stem: Package A (I=0.9) depends on package B (I=0.2). Both lie on the Main Sequence. Is the SDP satisfied?

options.text: No — A should never depend on anything
Cannot tell without the abstractness values
Yes — A is more instable than B, so depending toward the stable B is allowed

questions.explanation: SDP requires depending toward lower I; A(0.9)->B(0.2) is fine (DesignPrinciplesAndPatterns p.23-24). (DesignPrinciplesAndPatterns p.23)

questions.stem: Why does the guide insist clean code is not 'mere style or aesthetics'?

options.text: Because style guides are legally required
Messy dependencies cause rigidity/fragility/immobility/viscosity, so clean code directly lowers maintenance cost
Because clean code is only about indentation

questions.explanation: The macro narrative ties messy dependencies to the four symptoms — clean code shrinks the impact set (DesignPrinciplesAndPatterns p.2-3). (DesignPrinciplesAndPatterns p.2)

questions.stem: By what mechanism does the guide claim clean code drives down the cost of the Impact Analysis phase in particular?

options.text: Fewer surprise dependencies shrink the estimated impact set, so analysis is cheaper
It removes the need for Concept Location
It makes builds slower but safer

questions.explanation: Clean code means fewer hidden dependencies, shrinking the impact set and easing Impact Analysis (guide overview; DesignPrinciplesAndPatterns p.4). (DesignPrinciplesAndPatterns p.4)

questions.stem: A maintainer favors large packages (fewer touched per change); a reuser favors small packages. Which principles do they respectively champion?
questions.explanation: CCP serves maintainers (larger packages); REP/CRP serve reusers (smaller) (DesignPrinciplesAndPatterns p.18).

questions.stem: The guide nicknames clean code the 'write the code well companion' to the rest of the course. What earns it that label?

options.text: The other lectures find and propagate change; this one makes the code being changed good
It replaces the change process entirely
It is only about unit testing

questions.explanation: Clean code complements the change-process mechanics by making the code itself good (guide overview). (CleanCode p.3)

questions.stem: Martin's chapter predates 'SOLID' yet covers all five letters. Which letter is supplied by the Clean Code deck rather than the principles deck?
questions.explanation: OCP/LSP/ISP/DIP are in the principles deck; SRP (the S) is in the Clean Code deck (CleanCode p.79).

questions.stem: Package X has A=0.5, I=0.5. Is it on the Main Sequence, and what is D'?

options.text: No — it is in the Zone of Uselessness, D'=0.5
No — it is in the Zone of Pain, D'=0.5
Cannot tell without Ca and Ce

questions.explanation: A+I=1.0 means it is on the Main Sequence with D'=0 (DesignPrinciplesAndPatterns p.26-27). (DesignPrinciplesAndPatterns p.26)

questions.stem: A package has Ca=1, Ce=9, and 1 abstract class out of 10. Compute I and A, then state its zone.

options.text: I=0.9, A=0.1 — on the Main Sequence (concrete-instable end)

questions.explanation: I=9/10=0.9, A=1/10=0.1, A+I=1.0 so it sits on the Main Sequence at the concrete-instable end (DesignPrinciplesAndPatterns p.23-27). (DesignPrinciplesAndPatterns p.26)

questions.stem: A stable, depended-upon package is concrete (A=0.1) and has I=0.1. What two coupling principles is it most at odds with, and what zone is it in?

options.text: REP (should be released) — Zone of Pain
SDP (should be instable) — Zone of Uselessness
SAP (should be abstract) — it is in the Zone of Pain

questions.explanation: Being stable yet concrete violates SAP and lands it in the Zone of Pain (low A, low I) (DesignPrinciplesAndPatterns p.24-27). (DesignPrinciplesAndPatterns p.27)

questions.stem: Why does the deck claim that even PARTIAL compliance with OCP can yield dramatic improvements?

options.text: Partial OCP removes the need for tests
Partial OCP makes the build faster
Each module made closed to modification removes a class of future ripple edits, compounding across the system

questions.explanation: Even partial OCP compliance 'can make dramatic improvements' by closing modules to ripple edits (DesignPrinciplesAndPatterns p.7).

questions.stem: A team applies the Special Case pattern, returns empty collections, and replaces error codes with exceptions. Which course-phase benefit does the guide most directly attribute to such clean error handling?

options.text: Cheaper Verification — small, testable units with a clear normal flow

questions.explanation: Clean, small units with a defined normal flow make Verification cheaper (guide overview; CleanCode p.69). (CleanCode p.69)

questions.stem: Given an LSP violation that a client patches with a typeid check, why does the guide call this a self-perpetuating cost?

options.text: The patch raises the instability metric to 1
The patch deletes all unit tests
The patch creates a package cycle each time
The typeid patch is itself an OCP violation that must be revisited for every new subtype added

questions.explanation: Such patches are latent OCP violations needing revision per new subtype (DesignPrinciplesAndPatterns p.11-12). (DesignPrinciplesAndPatterns p.12)

questions.stem: A design needs a detector to trigger actors anonymously AND must let those actors be created without naming concrete classes. Which two patterns together fit, and why?

options.text: Observer (anonymous notification) and Abstract Factory (quarantined creation)

questions.explanation: Observer gives anonymous notification; Abstract Factory quarantines concrete creation (DesignPrinciplesAndPatterns p.29-32). (DesignPrinciplesAndPatterns p.32)

questions.stem: Why does the guide describe the package-cohesion structure as something that should 'jitter' over a project's life?

options.text: Because packages must be renamed each release
Early, CCP (maintainability) dominates; as it stabilizes, REP/CRP (reusability) take over — so the grouping shifts
Because cycles must be added then removed
Because abstractness must oscillate between 0 and 1

questions.explanation: The cohesion tension means structure jitters: CCP early, REP/CRP later (DesignPrinciplesAndPatterns p.18).

questions.stem: A reviewer claims a 12-line function 'does one thing' simply because it is short. Why is length alone insufficient evidence?

options.text: Twelve lines exceeds the deck's hard limit
Short functions always violate the Stepdown Rule
Length is the only criterion the deck gives
'One thing' means the steps are all one level of abstraction below the name; a short function can still mix levels or responsibilities

questions.explanation: 'Do one thing' is about a single abstraction level, not just line count (CleanCode p.23-24). (CleanCode p.24)

questions.stem: Two packages each sit exactly on the Main Sequence: P (A=0.8, I=0.2) and Q (A=0.2, I=0.8). Per SDP, may P depend on Q?

options.text: Yes — abstract packages may depend on anything
Yes — both are on the Main Sequence so any direction is fine
No — neither may depend on the other under any rule
No — P (I=0.2) is more stable than Q (I=0.8); depending on the less-stable Q violates SDP

questions.explanation: SDP forbids depending toward higher I; P(0.2) depending on Q(0.8) is wrong despite both being on the Main Sequence (DesignPrinciplesAndPatterns p.23-24). (DesignPrinciplesAndPatterns p.23)

questions.stem: Why is the claim 'maximize stability everywhere' self-defeating for a system that must evolve?

options.text: Stability raises the abstractness metric uncontrollably
Maximally stable packages are the hardest to change, so a system of only stable packages becomes rigid; some instability is needed where change is expected

questions.explanation: Instability is desirable where change is expected; all-stable is rigid (DesignPrinciplesAndPatterns p.24).

questions.stem: How do the Three Laws of TDD and F.I.R.S.T. jointly enable a trustworthy Continuous Integration build?

options.text: They replace Impact Analysis with testing
They eliminate the need for a build server
They make the build slower but more thorough
Test-first plus Fast/Independent/Self-validating tests give a self-testing build whose green status can be trusted

questions.explanation: FIRST + the Three Laws are the micro-foundation of CI's self-testing build (guide; CleanCode p.74, p.77). (CleanCode p.77)
